perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the disclosure, HUCOM agrees to treat, and will treat, the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION disclosed to it as confidential until such time as the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION becomes publicly available through no act or failure to act on the part of HUCOM as evidenced by written documentation. HUCOM further agrees not to make any use of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION other than for the above-mentioned purpose(s) and will not disclose CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any other person without the prior written consent of VASCO, except that if HUCOM is a corporation, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION may be disclosed to a person within the company on a need-to-know basis.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the disclosure, HUCOM agrees to treat the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION disclosed to it as confidential. HUCOM further agrees not to make any use of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION other than for the above-mentioned purpose(s) and will not disclose CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any other person without the prior written consent of VASCO, except that if HUCOM is a corporation, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION may be disclosed to a person within the company on a need-to-know basis.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that makes the confidentiality agreement void if the information becomes publicly available through no fault of HUCOM weakens HUCOM's rights. This may violate trade secret laws, as these laws generally do not protect information that is already publicly known through no wrongdoing of the receiving party. Some states define trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the clause allowing for public disclosure without liability contradicts the UTSA definition of a trade secret, which requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable. If HUCOM is not protected when the information becomes public knowledge without their actions, the confidentiality requirements become overly broad and possibly unenforceable under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the condition that the confidentiality agreement is void if the information becomes publicly available through no fault of HUCOM. The Law Explanation states this omission contradicts the UTSA definition of a trade secret, which requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable. The altered contract broadens the confidentiality requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 2, though mostly unreadable, appears to be from a legal document, and the Law Explanation supports the conclusion that removing the clause could violate trade secret laws if the information becomes public through no fault of HUCOM.

perturbation.original_text: If no satisfactory arrangement is concluded between the parties, or if otherwise requested by VASCO, HUCOM agrees to return to VASCO any written disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION provided by VASCO plus any copies, notes, summaries or other materials derived from the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: If no satisfactory arrangement is concluded between the parties, or if otherwise requested by VASCO, HUCOM agrees to return to VASCO any written disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION provided by VASCO.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return 'any copies, notes, summaries or other materials derived from the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' weakens VASCO's protection of its confidential information. This may conflict with trade secret protection laws, which aim to protect all manifestations of a trade secret, including derived materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement to return copies, notes, summaries, or other materials derived from the confidential information, the contract creates a loophole. CUTSA protects against misappropriation, which includes unauthorized use or disclosure of a trade secret. Allowing HUCOM to retain derivative materials weakens the protection afforded by CUTSA because those materials could contain or reveal the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'any copies, notes, summaries or other materials derived from the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' directly contradicts the intention of trade secret laws, specifically regarding misappropriation. Snippet 1 defines misappropriation as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By allowing HUCOM to retain derivative materials, the modified contract increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or use of the trade secret embedded within those materials, effectively weakening VASCO's protection as the trade secret holder.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to the subject matter set forth above, this Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes any previous oral or written representations, understandings or agreements as to the above subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to the subject matter set forth above, this Agreement constitutes the agreement between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the standard 'entire agreement' or 'integration' clause, also known as a merger clause, which states that the current document is the final and complete agreement, may contradict the parol evidence rule under contract law. It potentially allows for external agreements and understandings to be considered, increasing ambiguity and dispute risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 2-202 - Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the 'entire agreement' clause contradicts the principles of the parol evidence rule embodied in UCC § 2-202.  Without this clause, prior agreements or understandings that were intended to be superseded by the final written contract may be improperly admitted as evidence, undermining the integrity of the written agreement. The omission weakens the certainty and enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The removal of the 'entire agreement' clause directly contradicts the parol evidence rule as described in UCC § 2-202. The original text ensured that the written agreement was the complete and final expression of the parties' agreement, preventing prior oral or written agreements from being used to contradict or supplement the terms of the written contract. The changed text, by omitting this clause, opens the door for the admission of parol evidence, potentially undermining the integrity and enforceability of the written agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that terms set forth in a writing intended as a final expression of agreement cannot be contradicted by prior agreements. Removing the entire agreement clause weakens this protection, making the contract more vulnerable to disputes based on external evidence.

perturbation.original_text: The CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is disclosed in confidence so that HUCOM may evaluate and use CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for the purpose of assisting VASCO in the commercial exploitation thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is disclosed in confidence so that HUCOM may evaluate and use CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'for the purpose of assisting VASCO in the commercial exploitation thereof' broadens the permissible uses of the confidential information by HUCOM, potentially violating trade secret laws. It removes the specific, limited purpose for which the information was disclosed, and weakens the restrictions on use and disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission weakens the protection afforded to the trade secret. Section 757 of the Restatement (First) of Torts addresses liability for the disclosure or use of another's trade secret.  By removing the clause that specifies the purpose of assisting VASCO in commercial exploitation, the contract allows HUCOM to use the information for any purpose, which could include competing with VASCO. This unauthorized use is a violation of the trade secret, as the disclosure of information for uses other than those specified when disclosing the information will often constitute a breach.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the phrase 'for the purpose of assisting VASCO in the commercial exploitation thereof.' This significantly broadens the permissible uses of the confidential information by HUCOM. The Law Explanation states that this omission allows HUCOM to use the information for any purpose, including competing with VASCO, which is a violation of trade secret law. The removal weakens the protection afforded to the trade secret. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the Law Explanation explicitly outlines the legal implication of this change, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not relevant. This constitutes a contradiction because it undermines the original contract's intent to limit the use of confidential information to a specific purpose, potentially violating trade secret laws by allowing unauthorized use.
